sentences.base_language: Park Jieun is a student who is about to graduate and is looking for work.
sentences.target_language: 박지은은 학생이고 곧 졸업하기 때문에 일자리를 찾고 있습니다.
sentences.romanized: Bak Jieun-eun haksaeng-igo got jeoropagi ttaemune iljari-reul chatgo isseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-기 때문에** = because of doing (verb + 기 + 때문에): 졸업하**기 때문에** (because [she] is graduating). **-기** nominalises the verb, and **때문에** (because of) follows: 바쁘**기 때문에** (because [I'm] busy). Compare: **[noun] 때문에** (because of [noun]): 일 때문에 (because of work).
**찾고 있다** = is looking for (progressive): 찾**고 있다** uses the **-고 있다** progressive with **찾다** (to look for/to find). Korean uses **찾다** for both 'to look for' and 'to find' — context clarifies: 찾고 있어요 (looking for) vs 찾았어요 (found).

sentences.base_language: Today she has an interview for an engineer position and feels very nervous.
sentences.target_language: 오늘 엔지니어 자리에 면접이 있어서 매우 긴장되어 있습니다.
sentences.romanized: Oneul enjinieo jari-e myeonjeop-i isseo-seo maeu ginjang-doeeo isseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**긴장되다** = to feel nervous (passive/intransitive form of 긴장하다): 긴장**되어** 있다 (is in a nervous state). **-되다** forms intransitive/passive verbs from Sino-Korean nouns: 준비**되다** (to be prepared), 결정**되다** (to be decided). The **-어 있다** adds the sense of being in a resulting state.
**있어서** = because there is: 면접이 **있어서** 긴장된다 (nervous because there is an interview). **있다** + **-아/어서** provides a factual cause.

sentences.base_language: In the morning, she took a deep breath in front of the mirror and told herself: "No problem — I can do it."
sentences.target_language: 아침에 거울 앞에서 깊게 숨을 들이마시고 "괜찮아, 할 수 있어" 라고 스스로 말했습니다.
sentences.romanized: Achim-e geoul ap-eseo gipge sum-eul deuri masigo "gwaenchana, hal su isseo" rago seuseuro malhaetsseumnida.
literal: it's okay, I can do it
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ 수 있다** = can / be able to: 할 **수 있다** (can do). **수** (way/means) + **있다** (exist) = 'the means exists.' Negative: **-(으)ㄹ 수 없다** (cannot): 할 수 없다. Very common: **말할 수 있어요?** (Can you speak [the language]?)
**스스로** = by oneself / to oneself — an adverb emphasising self-action: 스스로 말하다 (tell oneself), 스스로 결정하다 (decide for oneself). Compare **혼자** (alone, by oneself): 혼자서 했어요 (did it alone). **스스로** focuses on self-direction; **혼자** focuses on being unaccompanied.

sentences.base_language: She had eggs and bread for breakfast, and drank a glass of milk.
sentences.target_language: 아침 식사로 달걀과 빵을 먹고 우유 한 잔을 마셨습니다.
sentences.romanized: Achim siksa-ro dalgyal-gwa ppang-eul meokgo uyu han jan-eul masyeosseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**로/으로** expressing purpose/function: 아침 식사**로** 먹다 (eat as/for breakfast). **로** (after vowel) / **으로** (after consonant) has many uses: direction (서울로, to Seoul), means (지하철로, by subway), purpose (선물로, as a gift).
**잔** is the counter for cups/glasses: 한 **잔** (one glass), 두 **잔** (two glasses). **마시다** (to drink) → past: 마시 + 었 → 마셨습니다. Note the vowel contraction: 마시 + 었 → 마셨 (시 + 었 → 셨).

sentences.base_language: The office was in the city centre, and she went by subway.
sentences.target_language: 사무실은 시내에 있어서 지하철을 타고 갔습니다.
sentences.romanized: Samusil-eun sinai-e isseo-seo jihacheol-eul tago gatsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**타다** = to ride/board (vehicle): 지하철을 **타다** (ride the subway), 버스를 **타다** (take the bus). Korean uses **을/를** with 타다 — the vehicle is the object. Compare: **에 타다** is also possible for emphasis on boarding. **타고 가다** = go by riding: 지하철을 **타고** 갔다 (went by subway, lit. 'rode and went').
**시내** = city centre / downtown: **시** (city) + **내** (inside). Common urban vocabulary: **시내버스** (city bus), **시내 중심부** (city centre). Compare **시외** (outside the city): 시외버스 (intercity bus).

sentences.base_language: On the subway, her hands were sweating from nerves.
sentences.target_language: 지하철 안에서 긴장 때문에 손에 땀이 났습니다.
sentences.romanized: Jihacheol an-eseo ginjang ttaemune son-e ttami nassseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**땀이 나다** = to sweat, lit. 'sweat comes out' — **나다** (to come out/emerge) is used for bodily phenomena: 피가 나다 (to bleed, lit. 'blood comes out'), 열이 나다 (to have a fever, lit. 'fever comes out'), 눈물이 나다 (tears come, to start crying). The bodily thing is the subject with **이/가**.
**안에서** = inside (location of action): 지하철 **안에서** (inside the subway). **안** (inside) + **에서** (at/in — action location). The location of static existence uses **에**: 지하철 **안에** 있다 (is inside the subway).

sentences.base_language: She arrived at the company building and glanced at her watch: nine-twenty.
sentences.target_language: 회사 건물에 도착해서 시계를 보니 아홉 시 이십 분이었습니다.
sentences.romanized: Hoesa geonmul-e dochakaeseo sigye-reul boni ahop si isip bun-ieosseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)니** = 'when/upon doing, [I found that]': 보**니** 아홉 시였다 (when I looked, it was nine). **-(으)니** is used for personal discovery or realisation — what you found when you performed the action. Very natural in narrative: 문을 열어**보니** 아무도 없었다 (when I opened the door, no one was there).
**도착하다** = to arrive (Sino-Korean verb): 도착**해서** (having arrived, so). Time: **아홉 시 이십 분** = 9:20 — Korean uses Sino-Korean numbers for time with 시 (hour) and 분 (minute): 아홉 시 삼십 분 = 9:30, 세 시 반 = 3:30 (반 = half).

sentences.base_language: The receptionist said: "How can I help you?"
sentences.target_language: 안내 직원이 "어떻게 도와드릴까요?" 라고 말했습니다.
sentences.romanized: Annae jigwon-i "eotteoke dowadeurilkkayo?" rago malhaetsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**도와드리다** = to help (honorific): **도와주다** (to help, neutral) → **도와드리다** (to help, with humility/respect toward the person helped). **드리다** is the humble/respectful form of **주다** (to give): 드릴까요? (shall I give/do for you?)
**-(으)ㄹ까요?** = shall I? / shall we? — an offer or suggestion asking the listener's preference: 도와드릴**까요**? (Shall I help you?), 창문을 열**까요**? (Shall I open the window?). A very polite and service-oriented construction.

sentences.base_language: Jieun replied: "Good morning. My name is Park Jieun and I have an interview at nine-thirty."
sentences.target_language: 지은은 "안녕하세요. 저는 박지은이고 아홉 시 반에 면접이 있습니다" 라고 대답했습니다.
sentences.romanized: Jieun-eun "annyeonghaseyo. Jeoneun Bak Jieun-igo ahop si ban-e myeonjeop-i isseumnida" rago daedaphaetsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**저** = I (humble/formal): used in formal or polite contexts. **나** = I (casual). The choice signals register: 저**는** (formal I), 나**는/난** (casual I). In a business setting like a job interview, **저** is always correct.
**반** = half-past: 아홉 시 **반** (nine-thirty). **반** can replace **삼십 분** (thirty minutes): 세 시 반 (3:30), 열두 시 반 (12:30). **면접이 있다** = have an interview — **있다** expresses scheduled events: 약속이 있다 (have an appointment), 수업이 있다 (have class).

sentences.base_language: The manager, Mr. Choi, arrived, shook her hand, and said: "Nice to meet you."
sentences.target_language: 최 부장님이 오셔서 악수를 하고 "처음 뵙겠습니다" 라고 말씀하셨습니다.
sentences.romanized: Choe bujang-nim-i osyeoseo aksu-reul hago "cheoeum boepgesseumnida" rago malsseum-hasyeosseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Subject honorifics** in Korean elevate the subject: **오다** (to come) → **오시다** (to come [hon.]); 오시 + 어서 → **오셔서** (having come). **말하다** → **말씀하다** (to speak [hon.]). When the subject is senior (manager, elder), their verbs and even some nouns are elevated: 말 → 말씀 (words [hon.]), 집 → 댁 (home [hon.]).
**뵙겠습니다** = nice to meet you (very formal/humble): **뵙다** is the humble form of **만나다** (to meet) — used when meeting a senior. The full phrase **처음 뵙겠습니다** is the most polite first-meeting greeting. More casual: **만나서 반갑습니다** (nice/glad to meet you).

sentences.base_language: Mr. Choi asked her: "Why do you want to be an engineer?"
sentences.target_language: 최 부장님은 지은에게 "왜 엔지니어가 되고 싶으세요?" 라고 물으셨습니다.
sentences.romanized: Choe bujang-nim-eun Jieun-ege "wae enjinieo-ga doego sipeuseyo?" rago mureusyeosseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**되다** = to become: 엔지니어**가** 되다 (become an engineer). The resulting role takes **이/가** (subject marker): 의사**가** 되다 (become a doctor), 선생님**이** 되다 (become a teacher). Compare **이다** (to be, a current identity): 의사**이다** (to be a doctor).
**-고 싶다** (want to) → honorific: **-고 싶으시다**: 되**고 싶으세요?** (Do you want to become?). **물으시다** is the honorific of **묻다** (to ask) — irregular: 묻 + 으시 → 물으시다. Note: **묻다** (to ask) vs **묻다** (to bury) — same spelling, different meanings.

sentences.base_language: Jieun replied: "Because I love designing and building software to help people."
sentences.target_language: 지은은 "사람들을 돕는 소프트웨어를 디자인하고 만드는 것을 정말 좋아하기 때문입니다" 라고 답했습니다.
sentences.romanized: Jieun-eun "saramdeul-eul dopneun sopeuteuweeo-reul dijainhago mandeuneun geot-eul jeongmal joahagi ttaemun-imnida" rago daphaetsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)는 것** nominalises a verb: 디자인하고 만드**는 것** (the act of designing and making). **것** (thing/fact) turns a verb clause into a noun: 먹**는 것** (eating/what [one] eats), 하**는 것** (doing it). Essential for expressing likes, dislikes, and preferences: 좋아하**는 것** (what [one] likes).
**좋아하기 때문입니다** = because I like/love it: **좋아하다** (to like) + **-기 때문** (because of doing). Note the distinction: 좋다 (to be good — adjective; 이것이 좋아요 = this is good/I like this) vs 좋아하다 (to actively like — verb; 음악을 좋아해요 = I like music).

sentences.base_language: Mr. Choi nodded, smiled, and said: "Very good answers." He seemed very satisfied.
sentences.target_language: 최 부장님은 고개를 끄덕이고 미소를 지으며 "아주 좋은 답변이네요" 라고 하셨습니다. 매우 만족하시는 것 같았습니다.
sentences.romanized: Choe bujang-nim-eun gogae-reul kkeudeogigo miso-reul jieumyeo "aju joeun dapbyeon-ineyo" rago hasyeosseumnida. Maeu manjokhasyeoneun geot gatatsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**고개를 끄덕이다** = to nod, lit. 'to bob the head' — a fixed expression: **고개** (head/neck) + **끄덕이다** (to bob). Korean has many similar body+verb expressions: 고개를 젓다 (shake the head), 손을 흔들다 (wave the hand), 어깨를 으쓱하다 (shrug).
**-(으)ㄴ/는 것 같다** = seems / appears to be: 만족하시는 것 **같았습니다** (seemed satisfied). One of the most frequent expressions of inference in Korean: 비가 올 것 같아요 (looks like it'll rain), 피곤한 것 같아요 (seems tired).

sentences.base_language: After the interview, Jieun felt much calmer.
sentences.target_language: 면접이 끝난 후에 지은은 훨씬 더 차분해졌습니다.
sentences.romanized: Myeonjeop-i kkeunnan hue Jieun-eun hwolssin deo chabunhaejyeosseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**끝나다** = to end/finish (intransitive): 면접이 **끝나다** (the interview ends). Compare **끝내다** (to finish, transitive): 일을 **끝내다** (finish the work). Korean has many such intransitive/transitive pairs: 열리다/열다 (open), 닫히다/닫다 (close), 보이다/보다 (be seen/see).
**-아/어지다** = to become (change of state): 차분해**지다** (become calm). This suffix turns adjectives into verbs expressing a change: 따뜻해지다 (become warm), 행복해지다 (become happy), 나빠지다 (become worse). **훨씬 더** = much more — **훨씬** amplifies comparative change.

sentences.base_language: She left the building and found a café nearby.
sentences.target_language: 건물을 나와서 근처에 카페를 찾았습니다.
sentences.romanized: Geonmul-eul nawaseo geunche-e kape-reul chajatsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**나오다** = to come out / to exit: 건물을 **나오다** (come out of the building). **을/를** with 나오다 marks the place exited from — just like Japanese **を** with 出る. Also: **나가다** (to go out — away from speaker), **나오다** (to come out — toward speaker or neutral).
**근처** = nearby / the vicinity: 근처**에** (at/near the vicinity). **에** marks location with **있다/찾다**: 근처**에** 있다 (is nearby), 근처에서 찾다 (look for nearby — **에서** for action). **찾다** here means 'found' (successfully located) — context vs **찾고 있다** (looking for, ongoing).

sentences.base_language: She ordered a coffee, sat down, and sent her mom a message.
sentences.target_language: 커피를 주문하고 자리에 앉아서 엄마에게 문자를 보냈습니다.
sentences.romanized: Keopi-reul jumunhago jari-e anjaseo eomma-ege munja-reul bonaetsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**주문하다** = to order: 커피를 **주문하다** (order a coffee). In restaurants, **주문** is the standard word for placing an order: 주문 받으세요? (Can I order?, lit. 'Are you receiving orders?'). **문자** = text message (short for 문자 메시지): 문자를 보내다 (send a text), 문자를 받다 (receive a text).
**앉아서** = having sat down, and then: **앉다** + **-아서** (seated, and then sent the message). **-아/어서** here sequences the actions: first sat, then messaged. Compare **앉고** (and, without the sequence emphasis).

sentences.base_language: She wrote: "Mom, the interview is done! I think it went well for me."
sentences.target_language: "엄마, 면접이 끝났어요! 잘 된 것 같아요" 라고 썼습니다.
sentences.romanized: "eomma, myeonjeop-i kkeunnaseoyo! Jal doen geot gatayo" rago sseosseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**잘 되다** = to go well / to work out: 잘 **됐어요** (it went well). **되다** (to become/to work out) is highly versatile: 어떻게 됐어요? (How did it go?), 잘 될 거예요 (it'll work out well). The expression **잘 되다** is the go-to phrase for 'things went/went well.'
**-(으)ㄴ 것 같다** (past inference): 잘 된 것 **같아요** (I think/seem it went well). The modifier **된** is the past adjective form of **되다**. Compare: 잘 **되는** 것 같아요 (seems to be going well, present) vs 잘 **된** 것 같아요 (seems to have gone well, past).

sentences.base_language: Her mom replied quickly: "How wonderful! Tonight I'll make the fish you like most."
sentences.target_language: 엄마는 바로 "잘 됐네! 오늘 저녁에 네가 제일 좋아하는 생선 요리를 만들어 줄게" 라고 답장했습니다.
sentences.romanized: Eomma-neun baro "jal doenne! oneul jeonyeok-e nega jeil joahaneun saengseon yori-reul mandeureojulge" rago dapjanghatsseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**잘 됐네!** = great! / how wonderful! — a casual reaction to good news. **됐다** (did well/worked out) + **네** (exclamation particle expressing the speaker's impression). **네** (or **구나/군**) at the end expresses a realisation or pleasant reaction: 예쁘**네** (oh, how pretty!), 맛있**네** (oh, this is delicious!).
**-아/어 주다** = do something for someone: 만들어 **주다** (make for [someone]). **주다** (to give) attached to the verb te-form expresses a favour: 도와 주다 (help), 가르쳐 주다 (teach). **-(으)ㄹ게** = 'I will' (speaker's commitment): 만들어 줄**게** (I'll make it for you) — a promise or intention.

sentences.base_language: Jieun smiled, feeling wonderful. She sipped her coffee and looked at the street through the window.
sentences.target_language: 지은은 미소를 지으며 매우 행복한 기분이었습니다. 커피를 홀짝이며 창문 너머로 거리를 바라봤습니다.
sentences.romanized: Jieun-eun miso-reul jieumyeo maeu haengbokhan gibun-ieosseumnida. Keopi-reul holjakimyeo changmun neomeo-ro geori-reul barabwasseumnida.
literal: was in a very happy mood
sentences.grammarNote:
**미소를 짓다** = to give a smile / to smile — **짓다** (to build/create) is used for facial expressions: 미소를 **짓다** (smile), 표정을 **짓다** (make an expression), 찡그린 표정을 **짓다** (frown). **-(으)며** = while: 지으**며** (while smiling).
**너머로** = beyond / through (to the other side): 창문 **너머로** (through/over the window, to the other side). **너머** = the other side/beyond. Compare: 창문**으로** 보다 (look through the window as a means) vs 창문 **너머로** 보다 (look beyond/through to the other side — more evocative). **바라보다** = to gaze at / to look toward — more intentional and contemplative than just **보다**.

sentences.base_language: Whatever the result, she knew she had already given her best.
sentences.target_language: 결과가 어떻든 그녀는 이미 최선을 다했다는 것을 알고 있었습니다.
sentences.romanized: Gyeolgwa-ga eotteoddeun geunyeo-neun imi choeseon-eul dahaessda-neun geot-eul algo isseosseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**어떻든** = whatever / however it is — from **어떻다** (to be some way) + **-든**: 결과가 **어떻든** (whatever the result may be). **-든/-든지** expresses indifference to alternatives: 누가 **오든** (whoever comes), 언제 **오든지** (whenever [they] come). A very expressive concessive pattern.
**최선을 다하다** = to do one's best, lit. 'to exhaust one's best' — **최선** (最善, utmost/best) + **다하다** (to exhaust/fulfil completely). A fixed idiom: 최선을 **다했다** (did one's best). **-다는 것** = the fact that ~: 다했**다는 것을** 알다 (know the fact that [she] did her best).
